segments.s511-g89: چنین گفت is a fixed order meaning “he said thus,” with the adverb چنین before the verb گفت. کز is the archaic contracted form of که + از (or a poetic equivalent of که) introducing the content clause: مرگ خود is “my/one's own death” (خود as reflexive/intensive), and چاره نیست is a present negative copula meaning “there is no remedy/solution.” The clause literally: “that from/for my death there is no remedy.”
segments.s511-g90: مرا here functions as a possessor/indirect-object marker (“for me” / “my”) placed before دل in poetic word order (cf. دلِ مرا). دل پر اندیشه = “a heart full of thought,” with پر used adjectivally “full (of).” زین باره is a poetic phrase meaning “about this matter” (زین = archaic/poetic ‘از این’ or ‘this’ + باره = ‘occasion/matter’). نیست is the present negative copula, so the clause means the heart is full of thought and not concerned with this matter.
segments.s511-g91: مرا again marks the experiencer/possessor (“for me” / “my”) in front of the phrase; بیش ازین = “more than this” (بیش = more, از + ین where ین is the archaic/poetic form of این ‘this’). زندگانی is ‘life,’ and نبود is the past negative of بودن meaning “there was not / I had not.” Together: “There was no more life for me” / “I have no life beyond this.”
segments.s511-g92: زمانه is the subject meaning ‘time’ or ‘fate/fortune.’ نکاهد is the negative present/poetic form of کاهیدن “to lessen” (here “will not / does not lessen”), and نخواهد فزود uses نخواهد (future auxiliary ‘will not’) plus the archaic verb فزود “to increase/add.” The pair is a neither–nor statement: time will neither lessen nor increase [it] (i.e. his remaining life).
segments.s511-g33: This clause has the topical prepositional phrase 'به روم آید' (to Rome comes) followed by a relative clause 'آنکس که ایران گرفت' (he who took Iran). Word order is Verb (آید) after the prepositional phrase, a common poetic inversion; 'آنکس که...' functions as the subject ('he who...'). 'گرفت' is an ordinary past-tense active verb 'took/seized'.
segments.s511-g34: This conditional clause begins with 'اگر' (if). 'کین' is a noun meaning vengeance/hatred and is the object of the following verb. 'بسیچد' is a poetic/archaic or uncertain form here indicating 'does much' or 'multiplies' (the exact morphological analysis is variant in classical Persian). 'نباشد' is the negative subjunctive/impersonal 'it would not be' or 'there is no', and 'شگفت' is a noun/adjective meaning 'a wonder' — together 'نباشد شگفت' = 'it would not be surprising.'
segments.s511-g35: 'هرآنکس که هست' is a standard relative phrase 'whoever is' (هرآنکس = whoever; که introduces the clause; هست is the copula/existential). 'از نژاد کیان' follows with the preposition 'از' + noun 'نژاد' (lineage) + the proper name 'کیان' (the Kayanids / royal house), so the whole phrase = 'whoever is of the Kayanid lineage.'
segments.s511-g36: 'نباید' is an impersonal modal 'one should not / it ought not to' introducing the negative expectation. The conjunction 'که' links to the subordinate clause. 'از باد' here uses classical/poetic 'باد' as an optative/accidental force ('by chance' / 'by the wind'), not literal wind. 'یابد' is a third‑person subjunctive/present form 'may find / suffer' and governs the object 'زیان' (harm/detriment).
segments.s511-g37: 'بزرگان و آزادگان' is the direct object group 'the great (men) and the free (nobles)'; the object marker 'را' follows them. 'بخوان' is an imperative meaning 'call together' / 'summon' in this context (imperative of خواندن used in the sense 'invite/assemble'); note that a literal dictionary sense like 'read' would be less appropriate here — the verb functions as an order addressed to the speaker's agent.
segments.s511-g38: This is a series introduced by repeated 'به' (to/for) marking what the summoned are to receive/attend: 'به بخش' (with/for gifts), 'و به سور' (and to a feast), 'و به رای' (and to counsel/consultation), 'و به خوان' (and to a banquet). The repeated 'به' is a rhetorical/poetic listing device; each noun names a type of honor or gathering.
segments.s511-g39: 'سزاوار' is an adjective/phrase meaning 'appropriate/fit for'; it governs the noun phrase 'هر مهتری کشوری' — 'هر' (every), 'مهتری' (the foremost/chief; an honorific for a leading man), and 'کشوری' (of the country/realm). Together: 'appropriate for every chief of the realm.'
segments.s511-g40: This coordinate clause gives two imperatives: the first verb 'بیارای' (here contextually 'adorn/array/prepare') is poetically formed and is used as an instruction to honor or embellish; the provided lemma senses are not a perfect match for the verbal imperative usage, so the exact dictionary index is not available. 'و' links the commands; 'آغاز کن' is a compound imperative 'begin/do the beginning (آغاز) — do (کن)' meaning 'begin' or 'set up'; 'دفتری' (of/for the office/register) names what to begin (a registry/office).
segments.s511-g65: ببردند is a past-tense plural verb ('they brought/took'). هم here is an emphatic/sequential adverb ('also/then' → 'immediately/too'). در زمان functions adverbially ('at that time / at once'); نزد شاه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of / to the king' (نزد governs the noun شاه). Word order is typical classical Persian SOV with temporal/location phrases fronted.
segments.s511-g66: بدو is the fused prepositional clitic به + او meaning 'to him' (not the imperative). کرد is the past tense 'made/did' used here with نگاه as its object: 'made a look' = 'looked'. شاه is the grammatical subject (placed after the verb in this line for poetic order). از شگفتی gives the cause/attitude ('out of/from astonishment' → 'with astonishment'). نگاه is a noun 'gaze/glance' receiving کرد.
segments.s511-g67: به فالش بد آمد is an impersonal/evaluative clause: 'for the divination/omen it came bad' → 'the omen turned out ill' (آمد + بد = 'proved bad'). هم‌انگاه is an adverb meaning 'immediately/at once'. گفت is the past tense 'he said' (subject is understood from context).
segments.s511-g68: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that'. این بچه در خاک is the noun phrase 'this child in the earth' (در خاک = 'in the soil/ground' i.e. to be buried). باید is the modal 'must/ought to', and نهفت here is the main verb meaning 'be hidden/buried' (classical usage; verb appears after باید in normal Persian order). The clause expresses necessity: 'that this child must be buried.'
segments.s511-g69: ز اخترشناسان is 'from the astrologers' (ز is the poetic form of از). بسی پیش خواند combines بسی (here 'much/many' in classical usage) with پیش ('before/in advance') and خواند (past of خواندن). خواند here means 'he summoned/consulted' or 'he called in' (not only 'read'). The whole means 'he consulted/summoned many astrologers beforehand.'
segments.s511-g70: وزان is a resumptive conjunction 'and/from that' linking to the previous clause ('because of that'). کودک مرده is a noun phrase 'the dead child'. چندی is an adverb 'for a while'. براند is a present/epistolary-classical form of راندن (3rd singular) meaning 'he drives/keeps away' — here 'kept away/removed for a time.' Word order places the verb at the end as usual.
segments.s511-g71: ستاره‌شمر is the subject 'the astrologer' (literal 'star‑counter'). زان غمی = ز + آن 'from that grief' = 'because of that sorrow'. گشت is the past-tense verb 'became/turned' and سخت here is an adverb/adjective 'severely/deeply' modifying گشت (i.e. 'became greatly/very'). Together: 'The astrologer, from that grief, became deeply distressed.'
segments.s511-g72: بپوشید is the simple past 'he covered/put on/veiled' (پوشیدن) used transitively. بر marks the target of that action ('over/on/onto'), so بر خسرو نیک‌بخت names the direct object/target: 'over Khosrow, the well‑fated/fortunate one' (خسرو نیک‌بخت is a name + epithet). In context the verb implies he wrapped/covered the king (probably with a cloak or veil); the instrument is not stated.
segments.s511-g17: 'که' here is the conjunction/complementizer 'that/when' introducing a reported clause. 'آن نامه' = 'that letter' (demonstrative + noun). 'شاه گیهان' is a title-like noun phrase 'king of the world' (شاه + genitival epithet گیهان). 'رسید' is the simple past 'reached/arrived' and completes the clause.
segments.s511-g18: This clause gives a deontic result. 'ز' = 'from' (poetic), 'بدکام' = 'ill‑fated' (an epithet describing a person). 'دستش' is دست + enclitic ش 'his hand' (direct possession). 'بباید' is an older/periphrastic form expressing necessity ('must/ought'); followed by the past stem 'کشید' to indicate the required action 'to pull/withdraw' — together functioning like 'one must pull/one must withdraw'.
segments.s511-g19: 'ازان بد' = 'from/of that bad/evil' (ازان = 'from that'; بد = 'bad/evil'). 'که کردی' is a relative clause 'that you did' modifying 'بد'. 'میندیش' is the imperative 'think/reflect' addressed to 'you'. 'نیز' = 'also/likewise', adding emphasis that this reflection is to be done as well.
segments.s511-g20: Word order is compact/poetic: 'از اندیشه درویش را' puts 'from the thought of the dervish' before the verb. 'از' = 'from', 'اندیشه' = 'thought', 'درویش' = 'the dervish' (possessor of the thought), and 'را' is the definite/object marker attaching to درویش to mark it as the relevant person. 'بخش' here functions as an exhortation 'grant/give' (poetic/elliptical imperative), and 'چیز' = 'a thing/something' is the direct object. Overall: 'grant the dervish something from (your) thought.'
segments.s511-g21: 'بپرهیز' is an imperative 'refrain / beware'. The conjunction 'و' links commands. 'جان را' = 'your life/soul' with را marking it as the object. 'به یزدان سپار' = 'entrust to God' (به = to; یزدان = God; سپار = verb 'entrust/hand over' in the imperative or jussive sense). The segment gives two linked imperatives: refrain and entrust your life to God.
segments.s511-g22: This is a negative/limiting injunction in compressed poetic form. 'به گیتی' = 'in the world' or 'toward the world'. 'جز' = 'except', 'از تخم نیکی' = 'from the seed/origin of goodness' (تخم = seed/origin; نیکی = goodness). The final 'مکار' is an adjectival/predicative form 'deceitful' used as a prohibitive admonition (practically: 'be not deceitful' or 'do not act deceitfully').
segments.s511-g23: 'همه' = 'all/everyone'. 'مرگ' = 'death' — the noun that defines the fate. 'راییم' is a poetic/archaic form here tied to the clause (its dictionary entry in the data is mismatched); functionally the clause reads 'we are (or we have) death as our fate' and 'تا زنده‌ایم' = 'as long as we are alive'. 'تا' = 'while/until' and 'زنده‌ایم' = 'we are alive' (present).
segments.s511-g24: 'به بیچارگی' = 'in/through misery' (به marking the state or cause). 'در' = preposition 'in' reinforcing location/state. 'سرافگنده‌ایم' is a composite verb/adjective 'we are put to shame / we are ashamed/abased' (present plural); the clause states the social/personal consequence of the prior line.
segments.s511-g41: به نام is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning “in the name of.” The nouns بزرگان و آزادگان follow it in normal Persian order (preposition + noun phrase); both are plural and coordinated by و. This whole phrase functions as a dedicatory preface.
segments.s511-g42: کزیشان is the archaic relative + pronominal clitic meaning “from whom / of whom” (کز + -شان). جهان is the direct object “the world.” یافتی is a perfective past 2nd-person singular (“you found/obtained”), so the agent is an implied “you.” رایگان is an adverbial/postpositive modifier “gratis/for nothing” qualifying یافتی. Word order: relative phrase → object → verb → adverb.
segments.s511-g43: یکی را uses را as the definite/object/topic marker to single out “one person.” مده is an archaic negative imperative of دادن (do not give). بر + دگر + دستگاه is a prepositional phrase “to/onto another’s post/establishment” (بر = to/toward, دگر = other, دستگاه = office/court). The order is object (یکی را) → negative imperative → target phrase.
segments.s511-g44: کسی را again marks the object/topic “anyone.” مخوان here is a negative imperative form of خواندن used to mean “do not call/name” or more generally “do not proclaim” (the provided sense list treats it as a negative imperative). بر جهان specifies scope/area “over the world,” with نیز (“also/too”) emphasizing that the prohibition applies in addition to the previous clause; شاه is the predicate noun “king.” Word order: object → negative imperative → circumstantial phrase → emphasis → predicate noun.
segments.s511-g45: سپر کن is an imperative composed of the noun سپر (“shield”) plus the verb کن (“do!”), here meaning “make/raise/provide a shield” or “protect.” کیان را is the definite object (“the Kians/kings”), with همه quantifying them (“all”). پیش بوم is a locative phrase (پیش = before/in front of; بوم = homeland), indicating where they should be set. The order is imperative → object (with focus marker) → quantifier → locative.
segments.s511-g46: چو here means “if/when” (conditional), خواهی is 2nd-person present “you want/wish,” and که introduces the subordinate clause (“that”). لشکر نیاید is a negative present/subjunctive clause meaning “the army should not come/does not come” (نیاید is a negative present/subjunctive form). به روم specifies the direction/target “to/into Rome.” Together this gives a conditional: “If you want that the host not come into Rome.”
segments.s511-g47: سکندر is the subject placed at the start. چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” پاسخ بران گونه is a noun phrase: پاسخ “answer,” modified by بران گونه (بران = “on/thereon/with respect to that,” گونه = “kind/manner”), so “an answer to/of that kind.” یافت is the past 3rd-person singular “found/received.” The clause is a typical classical order: subject → temporal conj → object phrase → verb.
segments.s511-g48: به introduces the goal/direction “to.” اندیشه و رای are two coordinated nouns “thought and counsel/plan”; دیگر modifies them (“another/different”). شتافت is a past tense verb meaning “hastened” or figuratively “turned/moved to” — here it means he adopted or resorted to a different plan. Word order: preposition + coordinated noun phrase + modifier → verb.
segments.s511-g9: هرانکس = 'whoever/anyone' (indefinite subject). کجا here appears in a raised/classical position within the relative phrase; taken together with بد ز تخم کیان it means 'whoever is bad from the seed of the Kians' (i.e. anyone of Kayan lineage who is wicked). Word order is the classical noun-clause identifying the target of the command.
segments.s511-g10: بفرمودشان is past 3rd‑person singular of the honorific/courtly verb 'to command' with the object clitic -شان 'them' = 'he ordered them'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that', and ببندد is a 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive form 'to bind/fasten' used after تا to express intended action. میان is an adverbial 'between/in the middle' and completes the purpose clause.
segments.s511-g11: همه is the plural subject 'all/everyone'. روی را is the definite object marked by را ('their faces'), followed by the directional phrase سوی درگه 'toward the court'. کنند is present/plural (used here in narrative) meaning 'they do/they turned' — so the clause literally 'everyone turns their face toward the court.'
segments.s511-g12: ز بدها is a prepositional phrase 'because of/from the bad ones / bad deeds'. گمانیش is a possessed noun (گمان + -ش) 'their/his suspicion' (the clitic -ش refers back to the relevant person), and کوته کنند is verb + object: کوته (short, curtailed) plus کنند (they make/do), i.e. 'they shorten/curtail (the) suspicion.' Together: 'because of the bad ones they curtailed their suspicion.'
segments.s511-g13: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. این نامه is the theme 'this letter', and بردند is past 3rd‑person plural 'they carried/brought'. نزد حکیم is a prepositional phrase 'to the presence of the sage' — normal SOV order with the verb at the end of the clause.
segments.s511-g14: دل ارسطالیس is a genitival/Appositive construction 'the heart of Aristotle' (classical Persian often omits the ezafe). شد is the past of 'become' used resultatively 'became / was', and به دو نیم is an adverbial phrase 'into two halves'. The whole clause means 'Aristotle's heart became (split) into two.'
segments.s511-g15: هم‌اندر زمان is a temporal phrase 'at the same time / together at that moment' (هم‌اندر = likewise/at the same time, زمان = time). پاسخ‌نامه is 'answer‑letter/response', and کرد is the past 3rd‑person singular 'he made/did' used here as 'he wrote/composed'.
segments.s511-g16: ز مژگان تو گفتی is a compact poetic clause: ز + noun phrase 'from/with your eyelashes' (مژگان تو) and گفتی used as substantive 'what you said/your utterance' (i.e. the hint you made by blinking). سر خامه is a compound 'the tip/head of the quill (pen)', and کرد (past 'he did/made') means 'he set/put' — idiomatically 'he put on the pen's tip what you had signaled with your eyelashes', i.e. he wrote down the hinted words.
segments.s511-g73: ز + NP + verb: ز (from/of) marks the source of the question: “he asked of the astrologers.” بپرسید is a past-tense narrative form (classical Persian) meaning “he asked”; و گفت links that action to the following speech clause, here functioning as “and he said.” Word order is source → object → verb, typical in older Persian narration.
segments.s511-g74: That-clause introduced by که‌گر (a conditional/ concessive conjunction ‘if/should’). هیچ here functions as an indefinite ‘anything/ever’; ماند is the past/ perfective root meaning ‘remain / be left’; سخن is ‘a word / speech’; در نهفت is a tight prepositional phrase ‘hidden inside’ (در + نهفت). Together: “if any word remained hidden within...”
segments.s511-g75: هم‌اکنون is a fronted temporal adverb (‘right now’). ببرم is a classical jussive/first‑person verbal form ‘I will take / I shall cut off’ (ب + برد-stem); its object is سرانتان ‘your heads’ (سران with the 2nd‑pl possessive). ز + تن is the ablative ‘from the body.’ The clause thus expresses an immediate violent action: ‘I will now take your heads from your bodies.’
segments.s511-g76: نیابید is a negative/resultive verbal form from یابیدن (here ‘you will obtain / find’ with negation: ‘you will not find / you shall have no…’). جز is the exclusive particle ‘except’; کام شیران is a genitival phrase ‘the desire/appetite of the lions’; کفن is the noun ‘shroud.’ The idiomatic meaning: ‘you will find no shroud except the (hungry) desire of lions’ (i.e. lions’ jaws will be your only covering).
segments.s511-g77: This is a temporal/clausal setup: ستاره‌شمر names the person (the astrologer/topic), چون here functions as ‘when / as,’ and برآشفت is a past tense verb ‘became angry.’ شاه that follows begins the main clause’s subject (‘the king’), so the segment sets up: ‘When the astrologer became angry, the king…’ (note the topical placement of ستاره‌شمر before the temporal clause).
segments.s511-g78: بدو is the allative clitic phrase ‘to him’ (to that person); گفت is the past verb ‘said.’ کای is a vocative particle ‘O,’ and نامور پیشگاه is a nominal phrase ‘renowned one (in/of) the presence (court).’ Together: ‘the king said to him, “O renowned one before the court…’” — پیشگاه here signals address within the royal presence.
segments.s511-g79: تو is the second‑person pronoun ‘you.’ بر اختر شیر زادی is an ablative/locative‑style phrase meaning ‘of/under the star (اختر) of the lion (شیر) — of lion‑birth (زادی)’: زادی is a nisba/noun indicating birth/origin (‘of birth’). نخست (‘first’) is an adverb modifying the predicate that follows. The phrase describes natal/astrological origin: ‘you, born under the lion’s star, at first…’
segments.s511-g80: بر before a plural noun phrase here marks authority/position ‘over’ (i.e. placed above). موبدان و ردان names the groups over whom authority is held (mōbeds — Zoroastrian priests — and rēdān/radān, a class/office). شد is the past auxiliary/verb ‘became / was made,’ and درست qualifies it ‘rightly / properly.’ So: ‘(you) were properly made over the mobeds and the rādān.’
segments.s511-g81: سر کودک مرده is a noun phrase 'the head of the dead child' (Persian often places modifiers after the noun: کودک مرده = 'dead child'). بینی is 2nd-person singular present 'you see'. چو here introduces a subordinate temporal/conditional clause 'when/if/as', and شیر is the simile 'like a lion'. The clause as a whole is a subordinate 'if/when you see...' clause that links to the result in the next segment.
segments.s511-g82: بگردد is a 3rd-person-form of گشتن used here with a resultive/expectational sense 'will be/turn/become' (classical/poetic present/subjunctive flavor). سر پادشاهیت = 'the head of your kingship' (پادشاهیت is the possessive/abstract 'your kingship'), and زیر is a predicate/adverb 'under/beneath' (there is no copula; Persian often leaves the copula implied). Together: 'the head of your kingship will be under (it).'
segments.s511-g83: پرآشوب is a predicate adjective 'full of turmoil/tumultuous' placed before the verb for emphasis. گردد (گردیدن) is here 'becomes' (3rd-person present/subjunctive or future in classical style). زمین is the subject 'the land/realm', and چندگاه is an adverb 'for a while/some time' specifying duration.
segments.s511-g84: چنین 'thus/so' connects this clause to the previous. تا is the subordinating conjunction 'until', introducing the limit clause. نشیند is 3rd-person present/subjunctive of نشستن 'may sit / sits / takes a seat' (classical usage often reads as 'will take seat'). یکی is an indefinite 'one/someone', and پیشگاه means 'the court / the royal presence' (place where one 'sits'). Word order is typical Persian: conjunction + verb + subject + locative noun.
segments.s511-g85: ستاره‌شمر is the subject 'the astrologer'. بیش ازین here functions as a comparative 'more than this/than that' and with هرک ('anyone') yields the sense 'more than anyone (else)'. بود is the past copula 'was' (here indicating habitual/past disposition). The phrase states that the astrologer was (more than others) inclined to say such things.
segments.s511-g86: همی is a classical adverb signalling continuous or habitual action (here 'would/continually'). گفت is the past verb 'said' (habitual past in narrative style). و is 'and'. آن را marks the definite object 'that' with the object marker را. نشانه is 'a sign/omen' and نمود is past of نمودن here 'showed/made manifest'. The clause says he kept saying so and presented it as an omen.
segments.s511-g87: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'. چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is past tense 'he heard'. زان ('از آن') means 'from that' or 'because of that'. شد غمی uses شد (became) plus غمی (a noun 'grief') to mean 'became sorrowful' — a common Persian copular construction where شدن + noun expresses undergoing a state.
segments.s511-g88: به رای و به مغزش is a paired dative/locative phrase 'into his counsel and into his brain' (به + noun with possessive suffix = 'into his X'). درآمد is past of درآمدن used in the sense 'entered (into) / came into', and کمی is an adverb 'a little' modifying درآمد. The phrase means a small measure of concern or doubt entered his counsel and mind.
segments.s511-g49: This is a nominal phrase: 'بزرگان و آزادگان' (the great and the free) is followed by the enclitic/particle 'را' which here functions as a topical/emphatic marker attached to the phrase. The prepositional/genitive expression 'ز دهر' ('from/of the age') follows in the poetic order (z + noun) to mean 'of the age' or 'of that era.'
segments.s511-g50: This clause contains the indefinite 'کسی' marked by 'را' (here functioning as a definite/object marker), followed by 'کش' (an archaic/poetic element used here as part of a verbal/participial idea), the preposition 'از' introducing the source phrase 'مردمی' ('from the people'), and the copula 'بود' (past tense 'was'). The final 'بهر' indicates purpose or suitability ('for/for the purpose of'). Taken together the clause says there was a person taken/chosen from among the people for that purpose.
segments.s511-g51: 'بفرمود' is an honorific/past tense verb meaning 'he commanded/ordered.' The particle 'تا' introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'that'), and 'پیش او' ('before him') gives the locus; 'خواندند' here is a past plural verb meaning 'they were called' or 'they read/performed' — in this context it is best read as 'they were summoned/called before him.'
segments.s511-g52: The preposition 'به' plus 'جای' ('to/into place') and the adjective 'سزاوار' ('appropriate, deserving') form the adverbial phrase 'به جای سزاوار' meaning 'into a fitting place/position.' The verb 'بنشاندند' is past plural 'they seated/installed (them),' with the direct object omitted because it is recoverable from the previous clause.
segments.s511-g53: 'یکی' ('one' / 'a certain one') is the subject, followed by 'عهد' ('oath/covenant') as its object; 'بنوشت' is an archaic past-tense verb form (b- prefix + نوشت) meaning 'he wrote' or 'he had written.' The conjunction 'تا' introduces the intended/result clause 'تا هر یکی' ('so that each one...').
segments.s511-g54: 'فزونی' ('excess, more') is the object of the negative-stem verb 'نجوید' (here used in a jussive/optative sense introduced by 'تا' — 'would not seek'); 'ز دهر' ('from/of the age/world') specifies the domain, and 'اندکی' ('a little') is the limit/measure. Together: 'so that no one should seek more from the world than a little.'
segments.s511-g55: 'بران' ('on/thereupon') is an adverbial particle pointing back to the previously mentioned persons; it introduces the appositive noun phrase 'نامداران جوینده کام' ('the renowned ones, seekers of favor/desire'). Word order is noun + modifier (adjective/participle) in the typical Persian sequence.
segments.s511-g56: 'ملوک طوایف' is a genitive/construct phrase reading 'kings of the tribes/peoples.' The verb 'نهادند' is past plural 'they placed/established/appointed,' and the noun 'نام' ('name' / 'title') is the object — idiomatically 'نهادند نام' = 'they gave the name/title' or 'they installed as kings.' Thus the clause says they appointed those renowned seekers as kings over the tribes.
segments.s511-g25: نه at the start negates the whole clause. هرکس که شد is a relative clause 'whoever/anyone who became' (شد = past 'became'), followed by the object پادشاهی and the verb ببرد. ببرد here is a classical present/subjunctive-form meaning 'takes/obtains/retains' (used with the notion of acquiring or keeping the kingship). Word order is subject/relative → verb → object, typical of Persian poetry where the relative clause precedes the verb.
segments.s511-g26: برفت is a verbal form meaning 'went away' or 'departed' in context, but the provided sense list does not match this use so sense is marked -1. The conjunction و links the two actions; بزرگی کسی را سپرد is a passive/causative sequence 'he handed over/entrusted greatness to someone' with را marking کسی as the definite object. سپرد is past-tense 'entrusted/handed over.'
segments.s511-g27: This is a pair of imperatives: بپرهیز ('refrain / beware') is a simple positive imperative, followed by و 'and', then خون بزرگان مریز where مریز is the negative imperative 'do not spill.' The object خون بزرگان ('the blood of the great/elders') comes before the verb, with بزرگان in genitive relation to خون.
segments.s511-g28: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause: 'for/that'. نفرین بود is past tense 'a curse was/would be' (بود indicating past/existent state). بر تو is the prepositional phrase 'upon you', and تا رستخیز is a temporal phrase 'until the Resurrection'. Overall it gives the consequence/reason for the previous command.
segments.s511-g29: و دیگر is a connective meaning 'and furthermore / moreover'. که چون introduces a temporal/subordinate clause ('that/when/as'), here functioning like 'when'. اندر ایران سپاه places سپاه ('army') inside the locative phrase 'in Iran' (اندر = poetic 'in'). This segment is an initial dependent clause setting the time/condition for the main clause.
segments.s511-g30: نباشد here functions impersonally/negatively — 'there is not / there is no' (existential negative) referring back to سپاه; the phrasing then presents همان شاه ('that very king') and the locative در پیش‌گاه ('in the presence / audience-hall'). Word order places the negative verb before the nominal phrase, a common Poetic/older Persian arrangement; the provided senses for نباشد did not match the existential meaning so it is marked -1.
segments.s511-g31: The preposition ز ('from', poetic form) is repeated for emphasis before each geographic name — a standard poetic device (ز ترک و ز هند ...). سقلاب is an archaic/place-name term here listed among lands; چین is 'China'. The repeated و links the list items.
segments.s511-g32: سپاه آید is a simple present/future sense 'an army comes/arrives' with آید (come). از هر سوی is a common locative phrase 'from every direction/quarter' (سوی = 'side/direction' here), and هم‌چنین adds 'likewise/similarly', summarizing that armies approach from all those lands.
segments.s511-g1: بدانست is a past tense verb 'he knew/realized' placed first as is common in epic narrative. کش is an archaic/poetic agentive/relational element attached to the following noun (مرگ) to mark the actor/force (literally 'the puller/agent of death' or simply 'death' in effect). نزدیک شد is a past tense verbal phrase 'drew near' (شد as past/become). Together the clause means 'he realized that death (or death's hand) had drawn near.'
segments.s511-g2: بروبر is an adverbial/poetic intensifier meaning roughly 'straightaway/at once' or 'directly'; همی is the classical progressive/continuous marker (here giving an ongoing or immediate sense). روز تاریک شد is the main predicate 'the day became/darkened' with شد as the past auxiliary/verb. Word order places adverb(s) before the subject and verb in a compact epic style.
segments.s511-g3: بران is a demonstrative/adverbial linking back to the previous event ('thereupon'/'on that'); بودش is the past copula with enclitic -ش (literary) giving 'it was to him' → idiomatically 'he had' or 'he thought'; اندیشه is the noun 'thought' and کاندر is the fused relative/locative 'that/in which' introducing the subordinate clause کاندر جهان 'in the world'. The phrase functions: 'Thereupon he had the thought that in the world ...'.
segments.s511-g4: نماند is past 'remained/was left' and here forms the predication before the subject (a common poetic order): نماند کسی = 'no one remained' (an idiomatic negative sense results without an explicit negative particle). کسی in this idiom functions as 'no one'; از نژاد مهان is a prepositional phrase 'of the lineage/race of the مهان' (مهان is an archaic/poetic term for the great/noble ones).
segments.s511-g5: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'who'); لشکر is the subject 'army/host'. کَشد is a classical third-person singular verb here indicating the action 'would draw/march' (subjunctive/intentive sense after a verb of thought or purpose). جنگ را is the definite object 'the war' (را marks it), and سوی روم indicates direction 'toward Rome'. The clause reads 'that an army would lead/march the war toward Rome.'
segments.s511-g6: نهد is the past tense 'he would set/place' (here functioning as a verb of pursuing or bringing about); پی here has the sense 'in pursuit of / following' (نهادن پی = 'to pursue'); بران 'upon that' links back to the previous idea; خاک آباد بوم is a noun phrase 'that inhabited/prosperous soil/home' (خاک بوم = land, آباد qualifying it). The whole segment expresses follow-up action: 'and would pursue them to that prosperous land.'
segments.s511-g7: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; مغز (literally 'brain') is used figuratively 'his mind' or 'counsel'; اندرین کار 'to this affair/plan' with خودکامه qualifying کار as 'self-willed/autocratic'. کرد is the past tense verb 'did/made' so the clause reads 'when he put his mind to this autocratic undertaking.'
segments.s511-g8: هم‌انگه is an adverb 'at that moment/then' (immediately). سطالیس را نامه کرد uses را as the object marker and نامه + کرد as a light‑verb construction meaning 'wrote/sent a letter to Satalis' (کرد with نامه functions like 'to write/send'). The whole segment: 'he at once wrote a letter to Satalis.'
segments.s511-g57: 'همان شب' is an adverbial phrase = 'that same night' placed before the subject. 'سکندر' is the subject; 'به بابل' is a prepositional phrase marking destination (to/into Babylon). 'رسید' is the simple past (3rd person singular) of 'رسیدن' meaning 'arrived' (the copular/auxiliary is not needed).
segments.s511-g58: 'مهان را' is the definite object/topic marked by 'را' ('the guests'). 'به دیدار خود' is a prepositional phrase literally 'to/for the meeting of himself' = 'to see him' (self-reference to the subject Alexander). 'شاد' is an adjective placed before the verb and functions as a predicative complement ('happy/glad'). 'دید' is the past tense verb 'saw' used here in the sense 'found/observed (them)'.
segments.s511-g59: The clause begins with 'یکی' used as an indefinite 'a certain' before 'کودک' ('a child came'), with the verb 'آمد' in past tense. The following phrase 'زنی را به شب' places 'زنی' ('a woman') with the object/topic marker 'را' and the temporal adverbial 'به شب' ('at night'), preparing for the following verb; word order is poetic/elliptical but normal in classical Persian.
segments.s511-g60: 'بدو' = 'to him/unto him' (indirect object pronoun phrase) and 'ماند' is the past of 'ماندن' ('remained, stayed'), so 'زنی را به شب بدو ماند' = 'a woman stayed with him that night.' The following relative clause 'هرکس که دیدش عجب' is 'whoever saw him (هرکس که دیدش) [found it] a wonder' — 'دیدش' combines verb + enclitic object 'ش' = 'saw him', and 'عجب' is a nominal predicate meaning 'astonishment/wonder'.
segments.s511-g61: 'سرش' = 'his head' (سر + possessive clitic ش). 'چون' introduces a simile: 'چون سر شیر' = 'like the head of a lion.' The clause 'بر پای سم' is a locative construction with 'بر' ('on/upon') + 'پای' ('foot') followed by the noun 'سم' ('hoof') as the predicative element; the copula is omitted (typical in Persian descriptive clauses).
segments.s511-g62: 'چو' (variant of 'چون') again introduces simile: 'چو مردم بر و کتف' is a compact/poetic order meaning '(his) shoulders like those of men' — here 'مردم' (men/people) + 'بر' (on) + 'کتف' (shoulder) produce the image 'on (his) shoulders like men’s.' The final phrase 'و چون گاو دم' = 'and like a cow, (his) tail' (again copula omitted). The sentence uses parallel similes and elides the verb 'to be'.
segments.s511-g63: 'بمرد' here expresses a potential/optative sense 'one might die' (literally 'may one die' or 'one would die') used hyperbolically. 'از شگفتی' = 'from astonishment.' 'هم‌آنگه که زاد' = 'just then/at that very moment that it was born' ('هم‌آنگه' = 'at that same instant', 'زاد' = past of 'زادن' = 'was born'). Together: 'one would die of astonishment as soon as it was born.'
segments.s511-g64: This is a conditional/adversative clause: 'سزدگر' is formed from 'سزد' (to be fitting) plus the conditional particle 'گر' = 'if' (so effectively 'if it were fitting/if it were right'), and 'نباشد' is the negative subjunctive/optative 'were not' — together 'سزدگر نباشد' = 'if it were not fitting.' 'ازان زن نژاد' = 'from that woman's lineage' (literally 'from that woman's race'). The whole gives '... if such offspring were not fitting of that woman's lineage.'

sentences.s511-s45: He said thus: there is no remedy for my death; my heart is full of thought and is not concerned with this matter.
sentences.s511-s46: I have no life beyond this; time will neither lessen it nor will it add to it.
sentences.s511-s17: He who took Iran will go to Rome; if he wreaks much vengeance, it will not be surprising.
sentences.s511-s18: Whoever belongs to the lineage of the Kayanids should not, by chance, suffer harm.
sentences.s511-s19: Summon the grandees and the free, and call them to gifts, to feasts, to counsel, and to a banquet.
sentences.s511-s20: Adorn (suitably) every chief of the realm, and set up/begin an office (register).
sentences.s511-s33: They immediately brought him into the king's presence; the king directed a gaze to him in astonishment.
sentences.s511-s34: The omen proved bad; immediately he said that this child must be buried in the earth.
sentences.s511-s35: He consulted many astrologers beforehand, and because of that he kept the dead child away for a while.
sentences.s511-s36: The astrologer, because of that sorrow, became deeply troubled and covered the fortunate king (with a cloak/his robe).
sentences.s511-s9: When that letter reached the king of the world, his hand must be withdrawn from the ill‑fated.
sentences.s511-s10: Think also on that evil you did; and from the dervish's thought grant (him) something.
sentences.s511-s11: Refrain (beware), and entrust your life to God; in the world do nothing except from the seed of goodness — be not deceitful.
sentences.s511-s12: We are all subject to death while we live; in our misery we are put to shame.
sentences.s511-s21: In the name of the great and the free, from whom you obtained the world gratis.
sentences.s511-s22: Do not give one (person) a post to another; do not call anyone king over the world either.
sentences.s511-s23: Make a shield: place all the Kians before the homeland, if you wish that the army not come into Rome.
sentences.s511-s24: When Alexander received an answer of that kind, he turned to another thought and counsel.
sentences.s511-s5: He ordered that anyone of Kayan stock who proved wicked be bound in the middle.
sentences.s511-s6: All turned their faces toward the royal court; because of the evildoers they curtailed their suspicions.
sentences.s511-s7: When this letter was brought to the sage, Aristotle's heart was split in two.
sentences.s511-s8: At the same time he composed an answer; he put on the pen's tip what you had hinted with your eyelashes.
sentences.s511-s37: He asked the astrologers and said, "If ever any word remained hidden within..."
sentences.s511-s38: At once I will take your heads from your bodies; you will find no shroud except the appetite of lions.
sentences.s511-s39: When the astrologer grew angry, the king said to him, “O famed one before the court...”
sentences.s511-s40: You were first of lion‑birth under the star, and properly were made over the mobeds and the rādān.
sentences.s511-s41: If you see the dead child's head turn like a lion, the head of your kingship will lie beneath (it).
sentences.s511-s42: The land will become tumultuous for a while, thus until at last someone takes a seat in the court.
sentences.s511-s43: The astrologer, more than anyone else, used to say such things, and he showed it as a sign.
sentences.s511-s44: When Alexander heard that, sorrow came upon him; a little concern entered his counsel and his mind.
sentences.s511-s25: Among the age's great and free there was one drawn from the people for the purpose.
sentences.s511-s26: He ordered that they be called before him; they read (or were called) and were seated in their proper places.
sentences.s511-s27: One wrote a covenant so that each one would not seek excess from the world beyond a little.
sentences.s511-s28: Upon them the famed seekers of favor — they named (or appointed) them kings over the tribes.
sentences.s511-s13: Not everyone who becomes king will keep the kingship; he departs and hands greatness over to someone.
sentences.s511-s14: Beware, and do not spill the blood of the great, for a curse would lie upon you until the Resurrection.
sentences.s511-s15: And furthermore, when there is no army in Iran, that same king (will be) in the audience-hall.
sentences.s511-s16: Armies likewise come from the Turks, from India, from Sklav and from China, from every quarter.
sentences.s511-s1: He realized that death had drawn near; immediately the day grew dark.
sentences.s511-s2: On that he thought that in the world no one of the noble line remained.
sentences.s511-s3: that an army would march to war against Rome and pursue them into that prosperous land.
sentences.s511-s4: When he set his mind to this autocratic plan, he at once wrote (sent) a letter to Satalis.
sentences.s511-s29: That same night Alexander arrived in Babylon and found the guests glad to see him.
sentences.s511-s30: A child came; that night a woman stayed with him — whoever saw him was amazed.
sentences.s511-s31: His head was like a lion's head, and on his foot a hoof; his shoulders like those of men, and his tail like a cow's.
sentences.s511-s32: One would have died of astonishment the very moment it was born — if such an offspring were not fitting for that woman's lineage.
